title: Psalms 57
subtitle: Be Exalted, O God, Above the Heavens

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “In the Shadow of Your Wings”

heb.paragraph: "Have mercy on me, my God, have mercy on me, for in you my soul takes refuge. I will take refuge in the shadow of your wings (kənāfek̅ā) until the disaster has passed" (v.1). Written in the cave (1 Sam 22:1 or 24:3). The cave becomes a temple; God’s wings become the shelter. The refrain: "Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; let your glory be over all the earth" (v.5, 11).

notes.note: "I will take refuge in the shadow of your wings until the disaster has passed." Temporary shelter for a temporary storm. The wings provide cover UNTIL the danger passes.
"I am in the midst of lions; my teeth are spears and arrows, their tongues sharp swords." Surrounded by predators whose weapons are speech.
"Be exalted, O God, above the heavens." The refrain. From the cave, David looks up — past the lions, past the danger, to God’s exaltation.

heb.paragraph: "My heart is steadfast (nāk̅ōn), O God, my heart is steadfast; I will sing and make music" (v.7). Steadfastness produces song. The enemies set a net, but fell into it themselves (v.6, the boomerang principle). David sings, plays the lyre, and wakes the dawn (v.8). The psalm ends with the refrain: "Be exalted above the heavens; let your glory be over all the earth" (v.11).

notes.note: "They spread a net for my feet — but they have fallen into it themselves." The boomerang principle from Psalms 7, 9, 35.
"My heart is steadfast. I will sing and make music. Awake, my soul! I will awaken the dawn." The musician-king: David’s art is worship. He sings before the sun rises — his praise precedes the light.
"Be exalted above the heavens; let your glory be over all the earth." The final refrain. From a cave, David prays for God’s glory to fill the cosmos.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Cave-dwelling fugitive whose praise rises above danger (superscription, vv.1-4,7-11)
ppl.text: The superscription says David 'fled from Saul into the cave' (1 Sam 22:1 or 24:3). From literal darkness and confinement, David produces one of the Psalter's most expansive praise psalms: 'Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; let your glory be over all the earth' (vv.5,11). He lies 'among ravenous beasts — among lions' whose 'teeth are spears and arrows' (v.4) — whether literal Philistines or Saul's soldiers, David is surrounded. Yet his 'heart is steadfast' (v.7) and he resolves to 'awaken the dawn' with worship (v.8) — praise preceding deliverance, not following it.

ppl.name: God (Elohim / Most High)
ppl.role: Refuge under whose wings David shelters (vv.1-3,5,10-11)
ppl.text: God's 'faithfulness reaches to the skies' (v.10) and David takes refuge 'in the shadow of your wings' (v.1) — maternal bird imagery also found in Deuteronomy 32:11 and Ruth 2:12. God 'sends from heaven and saves me' (v.3), dispatching his chesed (love) and emet (faithfulness) as personified agents of rescue. The refrain asking God to be 'exalted above the heavens' (vv.5,11) means David wants God's glory to be visible beyond his immediate crisis — personal deliverance serving cosmic doxology.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe sends from heaven and saves me, rebuking those who hotly pursue me— God sends forth his love and his faithfulness.ESVHe will send from heaven and save me; he will put to shame him who tramples on me. Selah God will send out his steadfast love and his faithfulness!NIVI will praise you, Lord, among the nations; I will sing of you among the peoples.ESVI will give thanks to you, O Lord, among the peoples; I will sing praises to you among the nations.

rec.text: ""I will take refuge in the shadow of your wings until the disaster has passed." Temporary shelter for a temporary storm. The wings provide cover UNTIL the danger passes."

rows.label: The Cave — 57:1–5
rows.text: "In the shadow of your wings." Refuge in the cave. Lions with sword-tongues. "Be exalted!"

hebtext.note: "Have mercy on me, my God, have mercy on me, for in you my soul takes refuge. I will take refuge in the shadow of your wings (kənāfek̅ā) until the disaster has passed" (v.1). Written in the cave (1 Sam 22:1 or 24:3). The cave becomes a temple; God’s wings become the shelter. The refrain: "Be exalted, O God, above the heavens; let your glory be over all the earth" (v.5, 11).

"My heart is steadfast (nāk̅ōn), O God, my heart is steadfast; I will sing and make music" (v.7). Steadfastness produces song. The enemies set a net, but fell into it themselves (v.6, the boomerang principle). David sings, plays the lyre, and wakes the dawn (v.8). The psalm ends with the refrain: "Be exalted above the heavens; let your glory be over all the earth" (v.11).

themes.note: Worship peaks: singing in the cave, awakening the dawn. Faith: steadfast heart amid danger. Sovereignty: "be exalted above the heavens."
